title: do-while Loop
description: The one loop that runs its body at least once before checking the condition — classic use case: menu-driven programs.
objectives: Write a do-while loop and know exactly how it differs from while
Recognize the "run at least once" guarantee do-while provides
Know when do-while is the natural choice

sections.text: In the while lesson, we saw that a while loop's condition is checked BEFORE the body runs — so the body can run zero times. do-while flips the order: the body runs first, and the condition is checked AFTER. That means a do-while loop's body always runs at least once, no matter what.
A loop that runs its body once, then repeats it as long as the condition (checked at the end, after the body) remains true — guaranteeing at least one execution.
Notice the semicolon after while (i <= 5);. do-while is the only loop in Java that ends with a semicolon after its condition — a common syntax mistake is forgetting it.

Compare to the equivalent while loop from the previous lesson — with the same starting condition (i = 10, checking i <= 5), a while loop prints nothing at all, but this do-while prints 10 once before checking and stopping. This is the entire reason do-while exists.

A very common real pattern: show a menu, do something based on the user's choice, and repeat until they choose to exit. You always want to show the menu at least once — which is exactly do-while's guarantee.

The menu must be shown at least once so the user has something to respond to — a plain while loop checking a condition before anything is printed wouldn't naturally support that first display without extra setup.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: num=5789: digitCount=1, num becomes 578 (5789/10, integer division)
num == 0 now, so the condition num != 0 is false — loop ends with digitCount = 4

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the semicolon after the while (condition) — do-while is the only loop that requires one there.
Using do-while when the body genuinely should be skippable — if there's any chance the body shouldn't run at all, while is the correct choice, not do-while.
Confusing the check timing — do-while checks AFTER the body, not before; assuming otherwise leads to wrong iteration-count predictions.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the key difference between while and do-while?
questions.answer: while checks its condition before running the body, so the body can run zero times. do-while runs the body first and checks the condition afterward, guaranteeing the body executes at least once.

questions.question: Why does do-while require a semicolon after its condition?
questions.answer: Because the while (condition) at the end of a do-while is a statement terminator, not the start of a new block — Java's grammar requires the semicolon to close the do-while statement, unlike a regular while loop's header.

questions.question: Give a real example where do-while is more natural than while.
questions.answer: A menu-driven program where the menu must be displayed at least once before the user can make any choice that determines whether to continue looping.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: do-while is like while, but with the condition check moved to the end instead of the beginning — the loop body runs at least once no matter what, since there's no check before the first execution. This makes it the natural fit for menu-driven programs, where you always want to show the menu at least once before deciding, based on the user's input, whether to loop again. Syntactically, do-while is the only loop that ends with a required semicolon after its condition, which is a common thing to forget.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: do-while = DO it first, ask WHILE later. It's the only loop guaranteed to run at least once, and the only one that ends with a semicolon.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the key difference between while and do-while?
How many times does a do-while body run at minimum?
Why does do-while need a semicolon at the end?
Name a real use case where do-while fits better than while.
